perturbation.original_text: (gg) "Trademarks" means any trademarks, service marks, trade names, trade dress, and other similar designations of source or origin, and registrations and applications for any of the foregoing.
perturbation.changed_text: (gg) "Trademarks" means any word, symbol, or design, or a combination thereof, used to identify and distinguish the goods of one party from those of others, excluding service marks and trade names.
perturbation.explanation: By explicitly excluding service marks and trade names from the definition of "Trademarks," the contract introduces a contradiction. Trademark law often encompasses service marks and trade names as forms of trademarks.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 15 U.S. Code § 1127 - Construction and definitions; intent of chapter
perturbation.law_explanation: The Lanham Act, specifically 15 U.S. Code § 1127, defines trademarks broadly to include any word, name, symbol, or device, or any combination thereof used to identify and distinguish goods or *services*. By excluding service marks, the modified contract contradicts this federal statute. The Cornell link points to a table of contents - click on Section 45 of the Trademark Act to see 15 U.S. Code § 1127.

Construction and definitions; intent of chapter In the construction of this chapter, unless the contrary is plainly apparent from the context- The United States includes and embraces all territory which is under its jurisdiction and control. The word "commerce" means all commerce which may lawfully be regulated by Congress. The term "principal register"
perturbation.justification: The modified contract text contradicts trademark law by excluding service marks and trade names from the definition of "Trademarks". The Law Explanation points to the Lanham Act, 15 U.S. Code § 1127, which defines trademarks to include goods *or* services. By explicitly excluding service marks, the modified contract deviates from this federal statute. Scraped Law Snippet 2 provides the U.S. Code citation 15 USC 1127, reinforcing the relevance of the Lanham Act and its definitions. This misalignment between the contract's definition and the legal definition constitutes a legal contradiction as the changed text fails to comply with the recognized definitions provided by federal law.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 Arizona Copyright Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a perpetual, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Copyrights for use in the Company Field throughout the world.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 Arizona Copyright Grant. Subject to the terms and conditions of this Agreement, Arizona hereby grants to the Company a revocable, non- exclusive, royalty-free license in, to and under the Arizona Licensed Copyrights for use in the Company Field throughout the world.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "perpetual" to "revocable", this contradicts the common understanding and legal treatment of perpetual licenses, especially in intellectual property. A perpetual license is generally understood to last indefinitely, while a revocable license can be terminated by the grantor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Campbell v. Loew's, Inc., 136 A.2d 815 (Del. Ch. 1957)
perturbation.law_citation: Campbell v. Loew's, Inc., 136 A.2d 815 (Del. Ch. 1957)
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware case law recognizes the concept of perpetual rights and licenses, but the change makes the right revocable, which creates uncertainty around the original intent of a perpetual and irrevocable transfer of rights.
perturbation.justification: The change from "perpetual" to "revocable" directly contradicts the nature of a perpetual license. The Perturbation Explanation highlights that a perpetual license lasts indefinitely, while a revocable license can be terminated. The Law Explanation suggests this creates uncertainty around the original intent of a perpetual transfer of rights. Because the modified text introduces a term that conflicts with the established meaning of 'perpetual,' making it revocable and thus fundamentally altering its legal effect, this creates a legal contradiction under the 'Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 10.1 Indemnification. Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party and its Affiliates and their respective employees, 9 Source: ARMSTRONG FLOORING, INC., 8-K, 1/7/2019 directors, officers, agents and successors (collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") from and against any and all losses (including all costs, liabilities (including present and future damages), claims and expenses) incurred or suffered by any of the Indemnified Parties, to the extent arising out of, relating to or resulting from (a) a breach by the Indemnifying Party of this Agreement; or (b) any gross negligence or willful misconduct of the Indemnifying Party in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.changed_text: 10.1 Indemnification. Each Party (the "Indemnifying Party") agrees to indemnify, defend and hold harmless the other Party and its Affiliates and their respective employees, 9 Source: ARMSTRONG FLOORING, INC., 8-K, 1/7/2019 directors, officers, agents and successors (collectively, the "Indemnified Parties") from and against any and all losses (including all costs, liabilities (including present and future damages), claims and expenses) incurred or suffered by any of the Indemnified Parties, to the extent arising out of, relating to or resulting from (a) a breach by the Indemnifying Party of this Agreement; or (b) any negligence of the Indemnifying Party in connection with this Agreement.
perturbation.explanation: By changing "gross negligence or willful misconduct" to "any negligence," the scope of indemnification is significantly broadened. This introduces a conflict because standard indemnification clauses typically require a higher threshold of culpability (gross negligence or willful misconduct) to trigger indemnification obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Under Delaware law, indemnification provisions are generally interpreted according to contract principles; however, statutes impose limits in certain contexts. For example, 8 Del. C. § 145 outlines indemnification of officers, directors, employees, and agents of corporations.
perturbation.law_explanation: While Delaware law allows parties to contractually agree to indemnification, 8 Del. C. § 145 places limits on indemnification in certain contexts (e.g., derivative suits) and typically requires a higher standard than simple negligence. By requiring indemnification for 'any negligence', the contract could be interpreted as exceeding what is permissible or commonly understood, especially in the context of corporate officers and directors.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Delaware Code Online Title 8 > Chapter 1 Authenticated PDF Â§ 141 Â§ 142 Â§ 143 Â§ 144 Â§ 145 Â§ 146 Â§ 147 TITLE 8 Corporations CHAPTER 1. General Corporation Law Subchapter IV. Directors and Officers Â§ 141. Board of directors; powers; number, qualifications, terms and quorum; committees; classes of directors; nonstock corporations; reliance upon books; action without meeting; removal. (a) The business and affairs of every corporation organized under this chapter shall be managed by or under the direction of a board of directors, except as may be otherwise provided in this chapter or in its certificate of incorporation. If any such provision is made in the certificate of incorporation, the powers and duties conferred or imposed upon the board of directors by this chapter shall be exercised or performed to such extent and by such person or persons as shall be provided in the certificate of incorporation. (b) The board of directors of a corporation shall consist of 1 or more members, each of whom shall be a natural person. The number of directors shall be fixed by, or in the manner provided in, the bylaws, unless the certificate of incorporation fixes the number of directors, in which case a change in the number of directors shall be made only by amendment of the certificate. Directors need not be stockholders unless so required by the certificate of incorporation or the bylaws. The certificate of incorporation or bylaws may prescribe other qualifications for directors. Each director shall hold office until such directorâs successor is elected and qualified or until such directorâs earlier resignation or removal. Any director may resign at any time upon notice given in writing or by electronic transmission to the corporation. A resignation is effective when the resignation is delivered unless the resignation specifies a later effective date or an effective date determined upon the happening of an event or events. A resignation which is conditioned upon the director failing to receive a specified vote for reelection as a director may provide that it is i
perturbation.justification: The modification from "gross negligence or willful misconduct" to "any negligence" broadens the scope of indemnification. The Law Explanation suggests this could exceed permissible or commonly understood standards, particularly for corporate officers and directors, under Delaware law. Scraped Law Snippet 1, referencing 8 Del. C. § 145 (although the snippet doesn't directly show that section), indicates potential limits on indemnification, supporting the argument that indemnifying for "any negligence" contradicts legal norms requiring a higher culpability standard. The change thus presents a misalignment with established legal principles concerning indemnification, creating a legal contradiction as the altered clause undermines standard culpability thresholds.
